_meta.authoringNotes: Global burden from GBD 2019 (Wang et al. Front Public Health 2022) and Hoy/Chambers Pneumonology 2020 review on engineered stone silicosis (PMID 31837691). NIOSH and OSHA exposure limits. ATS/ERS 2020 statement on silicosis. ILO classification of radiographs. ATS official statement on silicosis. Silicotuberculosis data from Knight et al. Eur Respir J 2015 (PMID 25593160).

heroOverview: Silicosis is an incurable, progressive fibrotic lung disease caused by inhalation of respirable crystalline silica dust at workplaces such as stone-cutting, quarrying, mining, sandblasting, foundry work, construction, and (increasingly) artificial-stone benchtop fabrication. Global Burden of Disease 2019 estimated approximately 23 million people exposed to occupational silica and over 12,000 silicosis deaths per year, with rapidly rising cases from engineered-stone (artificial quartz) workers in Australia, Europe, the United States, India, and Israel. Patients typically present with progressive breathlessness, dry cough, fatigue, and weight loss, often years after initial exposure. Untreated silicosis carries a high risk of tuberculosis (silicotuberculosis), progressive massive fibrosis, lung cancer, and respiratory failure. No treatment reverses the fibrosis; prevention through dust control and respiratory protection remains the only effective strategy, with lung transplantation as the last option for end-stage disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 23 million workers globally exposed to respirable crystalline silica; over 12,000 silicosis deaths per year (GBD 2019)
keyStats.demographics: Men account for over 90% of cases worldwide; engineered-stone silicosis appears at much younger ages (30-45) than traditional silicosis (50-70)
keyStats.avgAge: Traditional silicosis: 50-70 years after 20+ years of exposure; accelerated silicosis: 30-50 years after 5-10 years; acute silicosis: under 5 years from high-intensity exposure
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 0.5-2 million prevalent cases worldwide; rapidly rising incidence in countries with active engineered-stone industries

definition: Silicosis (ICD-10: J62.8) is an irreversible occupational interstitial lung disease caused by inhalation and pulmonary retention of respirable crystalline silica particles, predominantly α-quartz, cristobalite, or tridymite. Particles smaller than 5 microns reach the alveoli, where they are taken up by alveolar macrophages. Macrophages release inflammatory mediators (IL-1β, TNF-α, TGF-β), recruit more inflammatory cells, and ultimately die from oxidative damage, releasing the silica to be re-ingested in a self-perpetuating cycle. The result is characteristic silicotic nodules — concentric whorls of hyalinised collagen surrounding macrophage-engulfed silica — concentrated in the upper and posterior lung zones, with progressive nodular fibrosis. Three clinical forms exist: chronic (simple) silicosis appearing 10-30 years after exposure to ordinary dust levels; accelerated silicosis appearing 5-10 years after heavy exposure; and acute silicoproteinosis appearing within 6 months to 5 years from massive exposure (e.g., sandblasting). Coalescence of nodules produces progressive massive fibrosis (PMF) with conglomerate masses larger than 1 cm. Silicosis substantially raises the risk of tuberculosis (silicotuberculosis), non-tuberculous mycobacterial infection, autoimmune disease (rheumatoid arthritis, scleroderma, ANCA vasculitis, SLE), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, lung cancer (IARC group 1 carcinogen), and renal disease. Diagnosis requires occupational exposure history, characteristic radiology (ILO classification of small rounded opacities), and exclusion of mimicking diseases. The condition is managed by pulmonologists, occupational medicine physicians, and tuberculosis specialists.

typesClassification.description: Develops 10-30 years after exposure at typical occupational levels. Characterized by upper-lobe predominant small rounded opacities (ILO category 1 to 3, sizes p/q/r) on chest radiograph. Often diagnosed on surveillance imaging in workers without prominent symptoms initially.

Develops 5-10 years after heavy silica exposure. Engineered-stone workers represent the modern cohort, with cases reported in workers under 40. More rapid progression to PMF and respiratory failure than chronic silicosis.

Develops within 6 months to 5 years after very high-intensity exposure (sandblasting, tunneling, abrasive crystallization). Histology shows alveolar filling with periodic-acid-Schiff-positive proteinaceous material resembling pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, with rapid progression to respiratory failure and death.

Coalescence of silicotic nodules into masses larger than 1 cm, typically in the upper lobes with surrounding emphysema. Causes severe restrictive lung disease, traction bronchiectasis, and pulmonary hypertension. ILO category A, B, or C based on size.

typesClassification.type: Complicated silicosis with tuberculosis or autoimmune disease
typesClassification.description: Tuberculosis affects 3-30 fold more patients with silicosis than general population; non-tuberculous mycobacteria, ANCA vasculitis, rheumatoid arthritis, and scleroderma also occur. Caplan syndrome describes rheumatoid lung nodules in silica-exposed workers with rheumatoid arthritis.

primarySymptoms: Progressive exertional breathlessness developing over months to years, often noticed first on stairs or hills.
Persistent dry cough, sometimes with scant mucoid sputum, that may persist for years before formal diagnosis.
Reduced exercise tolerance with disproportion between symptoms and chest examination in early disease.
Fatigue, weight loss, and reduced appetite particularly in accelerated and acute forms.
Pleuritic chest pain, intercostal aching, or a sensation of chest tightness in advanced disease.
Haemoptysis raising concern for tuberculosis, lung cancer, or progressive massive fibrosis cavitation.
Recurrent chest infections including productive cough and prolonged recovery in chronic bronchitic forms.
Late-stage cyanosis, peripheral oedema, and signs of cor pulmonale from pulmonary hypertension.
Joint pain and morning stiffness suggesting Caplan syndrome (rheumatoid lung nodules in silica-exposed patients).
Skin tightening or Raynaud phenomenon in coexisting systemic sclerosis (scleroderma).
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent exertional breathlessness in a silica-exposed worker, even with normal spirometry
Small rounded upper-lobe opacities on a routine occupational health chest radiograph
Dry cough lasting more than 8 weeks in a stone fabricator, sandblaster, or quarry worker
Unexplained weight loss and fatigue in a silica-exposed worker
Positive interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) for latent tuberculosis in a silica-exposed worker
Decline in FEV1 or DLco on serial occupational health spirometry
emergencySigns: Acute severe breathlessness with hypoxia and respiratory distress in known silicosis — exclude pneumothorax, superimposed infection, or massive haemoptysis
Massive haemoptysis (>200 mL in 24 hours) — emergency bronchial artery embolization and bronchoscopy
Fever, night sweats, weight loss, and new cavitary lesion — investigate for active tuberculosis or non-tuberculous mycobacterial infection
Acute hypoxaemic respiratory failure in acute silicoproteinosis requiring intensive care
Right heart failure with peripheral oedema, raised jugular pressure, and severe breathlessness from cor pulmonale

causes.cause: Inhalation of respirable crystalline silica (α-quartz, cristobalite, tridymite)
causes.description: Particles smaller than 5 microns reach the alveoli; macrophages ingest the silica and die, releasing inflammatory mediators. Repeated cycles drive nodular fibrosis. Exposure intensity, duration, and silica content of the dust determine disease severity.

Artificial stone benchtops contain 70-95% crystalline silica, far above natural stone. Cutting, polishing, and dry shaping without adequate dust control produces extreme respirable silica exposure. Engineered-stone silicosis is a modern epidemic affecting workers in their 30s and 40s.

Hard-rock mining (gold, coal, lead, copper), quarrying granite and sandstone, and tunnel boring generate respirable silica dust. Underground workers face particularly high exposures from dry drilling and blasting.

Use of sand or silica-rich grit for abrasive blasting produces intense dust clouds. Many countries have banned silica-based abrasive blasting (e.g., NIOSH recommends substitutes), but exposure persists in informal sectors and globally.

causes.cause: Foundry work, refractory manufacturing, and ceramics
causes.description: Casting metals, fettling, manufacturing fire-resistant bricks, and processing ceramics expose workers to silica dust during sand moulding, grinding, and finishing steps.

Cutting, drilling, grinding, and crushing concrete, brick, mortar, and stone all release respirable silica. Power tool use without water suppression or local exhaust ventilation is particularly hazardous.

causes.cause: Inadequate respiratory protection and dust control
causes.description: Failure to implement engineering controls (water suppression, local exhaust ventilation, enclosure), administrative controls (rotation, training, exposure monitoring), and appropriate respiratory protection (powered air-purifying respirators for high exposure) drives the disease in nearly every reported case.

riskFactors.description: All forms of silicosis result from inhalation of respirable crystalline silica at work; risk is proportional to cumulative dose (concentration × duration). Engineered-stone workers, sandblasters, and miners carry the highest risk.
Artificial stone contains 70-95% silica versus 30% in granite. Accelerated silicosis has been reported after as little as 3-7 years of exposure, often in workers under 40.
Acute silicoproteinosis can develop within 6 months from massive exposure. Wet methods and enclosures reduce dust levels by 70-90%.
Smoking accelerates silicosis progression, increases COPD, and synergistically raises lung cancer risk in silica-exposed workers. Cessation reduces but does not eliminate elevated risk.
Long duration of exposure (typically 20+ years for chronic silicosis; 5-10 years for accelerated) raises risk substantially. Some workers develop disease after very short, very high exposure.
Pre-existing airway disease worsens functional impairment from silicosis but does not directly cause it. Smoking-related COPD is the most common concurrent disease.
Latent TB in silica-exposed workers has 3-30 fold higher progression rate to active disease. Annual TB screening with IGRA is recommended for workers and patients with silicosis.

affectedDemographics: Stone fabricators, quartz benchtop workers, sandblasters, foundry workers, miners, and construction workers carry the highest occupational risk.
Men comprise more than 90% of recorded cases worldwide because of male-dominated workforces in these industries.
Engineered-stone silicosis predominantly affects migrant workers in Australia, Israel, Spain, Italy, the US, and India, often in their 30s and 40s.
Low- and middle-income countries with informal mining and stone-cutting sectors carry the largest hidden burden of silicosis.
Coexisting tuberculosis affects 3-30% of silicosis patients depending on regional TB prevalence.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis requires three elements: a credible history of significant respirable crystalline silica exposure (occupation, duration, dust controls, respiratory protection), characteristic imaging changes, and exclusion of mimicking diseases. A detailed occupational history covers all relevant jobs, tasks performed, dust controls used, respirator type and fit, hobbies (stone-working, ceramics), and time since last exposure. The International Labour Organization (ILO) classification of pneumoconiosis radiographs grades small rounded opacities by size (p, q, r) and profusion (categories 0/0 to 3/+) using standard reference films. High-resolution CT of the chest is far more sensitive and shows centrilobular and perilymphatic small nodules, predominantly upper-lobe and posterior, with subpleural pseudoplaques, mediastinal lymphadenopathy (sometimes with eggshell calcification), and conglomerate masses in PMF. Pulmonary function testing typically shows restrictive or mixed restrictive-obstructive impairment; in advanced disease, gas transfer (DLco) is reduced. Bronchoalveolar lavage may show silica particles under polarised microscopy. Surgical or transbronchial lung biopsy is occasionally needed to exclude other granulomatous diseases. Annual tuberculosis screening with IGRA, sputum acid-fast bacilli, and chest imaging is essential. Connective tissue disease screening (rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP, ANA, ANCA, anti-Scl-70) is recommended. Lung cancer surveillance with low-dose CT is considered in selected high-risk patients. Differential diagnosis includes sarcoidosis (sharply demarcated upper lobe nodules with hilar lymphadenopathy), hypersensitivity pneumonitis, tuberculosis, fungal lung disease, lung cancer, beryllium disease, and coal workers' pneumoconiosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms exposure to respirable crystalline silica and quantifies likely cumulative dose
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-60 minute consultation covering every job, task, dust controls, respirator use, hobbies, and duration. Standardized questionnaires (such as the ATS Occupational Lung Disease form) are useful.

diagnosticTests.test: Posteroanterior chest radiograph with ILO classification
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies small rounded opacities, classifies profusion and size, detects PMF and lymphadenopathy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard chest X-ray in clinic; takes 5 minutes. Specialist B-reader or ILO-certified reader grades the films using reference standards.

diagnosticTests.purpose: More sensitive than radiograph for early nodular disease, PMF, emphysema, lymphadenopathy, and superimposed pathology
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contrast scan, 10-15 minutes. Performed at diagnosis and to monitor progression every 2-5 years. Identifies coexisting disease (TB, lung cancer).

diagnosticTests.test: Pulmonary function testing (spirometry, lung volumes, DLco)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies airflow obstruction, restriction, and gas transfer impairment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20-30 minute test in a pulmonary function laboratory. Performed at diagnosis and yearly. Serial decline guides therapy and disability assessment.

diagnosticTests.test: Annual tuberculosis screening (IGRA or tuberculin test, sputum AFB, chest imaging)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects latent and active TB; silica exposure raises TB risk 3-30 fold
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood test for IGRA; sputum collection for AFB; chest X-ray. Performed at diagnosis and annually thereafter.

diagnosticTests.test: Connective tissue disease serology (RF, anti-CCP, ANA, ANCA, anti-Scl-70)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for associated autoimmune disease (Caplan syndrome, scleroderma, ANCA vasculitis, lupus)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw covers all markers. Repeated if new musculoskeletal, skin, or renal symptoms appear.

diagnosticTests.test: Bronchoscopy with bronchoalveolar lavage or transbronchial biopsy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes infection or malignancy when imaging is atypical; may identify silica crystals under polarised light
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case procedure under sedation. Mild throat discomfort and cough for 24 hours. Results within days.

diagnosticTests.test: Low-dose CT lung cancer screening (selected patients)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects lung cancer earlier in silicosis patients who meet screening criteria
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Annual low-dose CT for patients meeting national lung cancer screening criteria (combined with silicosis as a high-risk factor).

treatmentOverview: No therapy reverses or halts silicotic fibrosis. The mainstays are exposure cessation, supportive care, treatment of complications, and aggressive surveillance. Immediate removal from further silica exposure is the single most important step; continued exposure accelerates progression substantially. Smoking cessation reduces decline in lung function, lowers COPD progression, and reduces lung cancer risk. Annual influenza vaccination and pneumococcal vaccination (PCV20 plus PPSV23) reduce infective exacerbations. Tuberculosis is treated according to standard regimens (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol for 2 months then rifampicin and isoniazid for 4 months); latent TB is treated with isoniazid for 6-9 months or rifampicin for 4 months, with treatment of latent TB recommended in all silica-exposed patients with positive IGRA. Non-tuberculous mycobacterial infection is treated with multidrug regimens guided by species identification and susceptibility. Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise capacity, dyspnoea, and quality of life in patients with moderate-severe impairment. Long-term oxygen therapy is offered when resting hypoxaemia (PaO2 < 7.3 kPa or 55 mmHg, or 8 kPa/60 mmHg with cor pulmonale) is confirmed by arterial blood gas, following the BTS and ATS criteria; portable oxygen for ambulation supports activity. Inhaled bronchodilators and inhaled corticosteroids are used for the airway-obstruction component (overlap with COPD or asthma). Pirfenidone and nintedanib (antifibrotic drugs licensed for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and other progressive fibrosing interstitial lung diseases) have been used off-label in progressive silicosis with limited evidence; results from emerging trials are awaited. Selected patients with end-stage disease are considered for lung transplantation, with case series reporting 5-year survival of 50-60%. Whole-lung lavage (under general anaesthesia) has been used for acute silicoproteinosis but is not standard for chronic silicosis. Disability assessment and compensation under workers' compensation, social security, or veterans' affairs schemes are an important practical task. Patient education on infection prevention, smoking cessation, dust avoidance (including in hobbies), and recognition of complications is essential. Engagement with occupational medicine, social work, and patient advocacy groups supports long-term care.

medicalTreatments.name: Immediate removal from silica exposure
medicalTreatments.description: Single most important intervention. Worker is moved from the silica-exposed task permanently and offered alternative employment with appropriate support.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Slows progression; cumulative dose continues to drive ongoing fibrosis even after cessation, but ongoing exposure markedly accelerates disease

medicalTreatments.name: Smoking cessation with pharmacotherapy and counselling
medicalTreatments.description: Behavioural counselling plus nicotine replacement, varenicline, or bupropion. Combined approaches yield highest quit rates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Slows FEV1 decline by approximately 50% and reduces lung cancer and TB risk significantly

medicalTreatments.name: Annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination
medicalTreatments.description: Inactivated influenza vaccine yearly; pneumococcal conjugate (PCV20) and polysaccharide (PPSV23) vaccines according to national guidelines. Herpes zoster vaccine recommended for adults over 50.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hospitalization for pneumonia by 30-50% in chronic lung disease populations

medicalTreatments.name: Tuberculosis treatment (active or latent)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard rifampicin-isoniazid-pyrazinamide-ethambutol for 2 months then rifampicin-isoniazid for 4 months for active TB; isoniazid 9 months or rifampicin 4 months for latent TB. Directly observed therapy considered.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Active TB cure rate >90% with standard regimen; latent TB treatment reduces progression to active disease by 60-90%

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled bronchodilators (LAMA/LABA) and inhaled corticosteroids
medicalTreatments.description: Used for overlapping airflow obstruction (COPD-like component) and chronic bronchitis symptoms. Tiotropium 18 µg daily, salmeterol/fluticasone, indacaterol/glycopyrronium are common choices.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves dyspnoea and quality of life in patients with reversible airflow obstruction or significant symptomatic component

medicalTreatments.name: Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) when criteria met
medicalTreatments.description: Continuous supplemental oxygen at 1-4 L/min via nasal cannula for at least 15 hours per day in patients with resting PaO2 ≤ 7.3 kPa (or 8 kPa with cor pulmonale).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality in chronic hypoxaemic respiratory failure; improves cognition, exercise tolerance, and right heart function

medicalTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation programme
medicalTreatments.description: 6-12 week supervised programme of exercise training, education, breathing techniques, and nutrition. Outpatient or community-based.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces dyspnoea, improves 6-minute walk distance by 50-100 m, and improves quality-of-life scores in 70-80% of participants

medicalTreatments.name: Antifibrotic therapy (pirfenidone or nintedanib) — off-label
medicalTreatments.description: Considered in progressive fibrosing silicosis based on emerging trials extrapolated from idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and progressive pulmonary fibrosis. Specialist supervision required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modestly slows lung function decline in progressive fibrosing ILD trials; specific silicosis evidence is limited

surgicalOptions.name: Lung transplantation (single or bilateral)
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for end-stage silicosis with respiratory failure, severe functional impairment, and no major comorbidities. Bilateral transplantation is preferred to avoid silicotic native lung complications. Lifelong immunosuppression and TB prophylaxis required.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 1-year survival 80-85% and 5-year survival 50-60% in published silicosis transplant series (comparable to other ILDs)

surgicalOptions.name: Whole-lung lavage
surgicalOptions.description: Performed under general anaesthesia with double-lumen tube; one lung is washed with saline while the other is ventilated. Used in acute silicoproteinosis to remove proteinaceous material; not standard for chronic silicosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant short-term improvement in acute silicoproteinosis case series; long-term benefit limited

surgicalOptions.name: Bronchoscopic mucus plug removal and bronchial artery embolization
surgicalOptions.description: Therapeutic bronchoscopy for mucus retention; interventional radiology for massive haemoptysis from PMF or TB cavities.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Controls acute haemoptysis in 80-90% of cases; rebleeding occurs in 20-30% within 1-2 years

alternativeTreatments.name: Pulmonary rehabilitation and structured home exercise
alternativeTreatments.description: Evidence-based supervised programme improves exercise capacity, dyspnoea, and quality of life. Home-based maintenance after a structured programme is encouraged.

alternativeTreatments.name: Psychological support and peer-group programmes
alternativeTreatments.description: Silicosis patients have higher rates of depression and anxiety, particularly with progressive disease and occupational consequences. Cognitive behavioural therapy, peer support, and counselling improve quality of life.

whySeeSpecialist: A pulmonologist or occupational medicine physician confirms the diagnosis, classifies disease severity, screens for tuberculosis and autoimmune disease, manages complications, and coordinates compensation and disability claims. Early specialist involvement is critical because exposure cessation is the most powerful intervention.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a centre with an occupational lung disease clinic, ILO-certified radiograph reading, access to high-resolution CT, full pulmonary function testing, TB clinic links, and lung transplant referral pathways. Familiarity with workers' compensation processes is important.
hospitalCriteria: Occupational and environmental lung disease clinic
High-resolution CT and respiratory radiology capability
Pulmonary function laboratory with gas transfer
preventionStrategies: Eliminate or substitute silica-containing materials where possible (water-jet cutting, alternative abrasives instead of silica sand).
Engineering controls: water suppression on power tools, local exhaust ventilation, enclosed cabs, downdraft tables.
Administrative controls: exposure monitoring, training, job rotation, written silica exposure control plans.
Respiratory protection: fit-tested powered air-purifying respirators (PAPR) with HEPA filters for high-exposure tasks.
Workplace medical surveillance: pre-employment and annual chest imaging plus spirometry for silica-exposed workers.
Annual tuberculosis screening with IGRA in all silica-exposed workers and treatment of latent TB.
Educate workers and employers about engineered-stone risks; ban dry processing of artificial stone where regulations permit.
Enforce regulatory exposure limits (OSHA: 0.05 mg/m³ over 8 hours; NIOSH recommended exposure limit 0.025 mg/m³).
lifestyleModifications: Stop work in silica-exposed environments permanently after diagnosis.
Stop smoking and avoid second-hand smoke; nicotine accelerates silicosis and raises lung cancer and TB risk.
Maintain annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccinations.
Adhere to pulmonary rehabilitation exercises at home between supervised sessions.
Avoid dust-generating hobbies (sandblasting, stone-working, ceramics) and use respiratory protection if unavoidable.
Maintain a healthy weight (BMI 20-25) to optimize respiratory mechanics.
Stay well hydrated to keep airway mucus thin.
Use medical alert identification and inform every clinician of the silicosis diagnosis.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet rich in fruit, vegetables, oily fish, and whole grains for cardiovascular and lung protection
Adequate protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to preserve respiratory muscle mass
Vitamin D and calcium supplementation when deficient, particularly on long-term steroids
Adequate hydration (2-2.5 L/day) to support airway clearance
avoid: Excess alcohol (>14 units/week), which interacts with antitubercular drugs and worsens nutrition
Smoking and exposure to second-hand smoke
Excessive salt that aggravates fluid retention in cor pulmonale
Highly processed foods low in antioxidants and protein

exerciseGuidelines: Encourage moderate aerobic exercise (walking, swimming, cycling) for at least 30 minutes 5 days per week within tolerance. Supervised pulmonary rehabilitation is recommended for patients with significant breathlessness. Pace activity, use bronchodilators before exercise if needed, and use supplemental oxygen during activity if prescribed.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed inhaled controller therapy every morning and evening.
Use supplemental oxygen for at least 15 hours per day if prescribed long-term oxygen therapy.
Perform pulmonary rehabilitation exercises daily and review with the physiotherapist regularly.
Track breathlessness (modified MRC dyspnoea scale) and report worsening promptly.
Monitor for symptoms of tuberculosis (fever, night sweats, weight loss, productive cough) and seek urgent review.
Stop smoking permanently with support from pharmacotherapy and counselling.
Maintain a daily symptom and activity diary to track disease progression.
prognosis: Outcomes depend on disease type, stage, ongoing exposure, smoking, tuberculosis, and access to care. Chronic simple silicosis without progression has slow decline and may be compatible with near-normal life expectancy if exposure ceases and smoking stops. Progressive massive fibrosis carries substantially worse prognosis with rising dyspnoea, cor pulmonale, and respiratory failure; 5-year survival is 50-70% in established PMF. Accelerated silicosis (especially engineered-stone) progresses rapidly with respiratory failure within 5-10 years in many cases. Acute silicoproteinosis has mortality of 70-100% within months to a few years without lung transplantation. Silicotuberculosis substantially raises mortality (3-fold) but is treatable. Lung cancer risk is approximately doubled in non-smoking silicosis patients and synergistically amplified by smoking. End-stage disease may be salvaged by lung transplantation with 5-year survival around 50-60%, comparable to transplantation for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. Disability and quality-of-life impairment are profound in advanced disease.
recoveryTimeline: Silicosis does not reverse. Exposure cessation may slow progression. Symptoms generally progress over years. Pulmonary rehabilitation produces measurable improvements in exercise capacity within 8-12 weeks. Tuberculosis is treated over 6 months with cure rates >90%. Lung transplantation requires lifelong follow-up.
complications: Tuberculosis (silicotuberculosis) — increased risk 3-30 fold; annual screening and prophylactic treatment of latent TB are essential.
Progressive massive fibrosis (PMF) with severe restrictive lung disease and respiratory failure — manage with oxygen, rehabilitation, and transplant assessment.
Lung cancer — approximately 2-fold increased risk; offer smoking cessation and low-dose CT screening where appropriate.
Non-tuberculous mycobacterial infection (e.g., Mycobacterium avium complex) — diagnose with sputum culture and treat with multidrug regimens.
Cor pulmonale and pulmonary hypertension — manage with diuretics, oxygen, and selected vasodilators.
Connective tissue disease (Caplan syndrome, scleroderma, ANCA vasculitis, lupus) — screen periodically and treat in conjunction with rheumatology.
Spontaneous pneumothorax and bullous emphysema — managed by thoracic surgery.

supportResources.name: American Lung Association — Silicosis
supportResources.description: Patient-friendly overview of silicosis and prevention from the leading US lung charity.

supportResources.name: CDC NIOSH — Silica information
supportResources.description: US occupational safety guidance, exposure limits, and prevention resources for workers and employers.

supportResources.name: OSHA — Crystalline Silica
supportResources.description: US Occupational Safety and Health Administration regulations, hazard communication, and enforcement information.

supportResources.name: Asthma + Lung UK — Silicosis
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing information, helpline, and support for silicosis patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asbestosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asbestosis is caused by inhaled asbestos fibres and produces lower-zone reticular changes, pleural plaques, and a different occupational history (insulation, shipbuilding). Silicosis affects upper zones with nodular fibrosis and is caused by silica exposure. Both are pneumoconioses but distinct conditions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pulmonary TB produces upper-lobe cavitary lesions with positive AFB or PCR and responds to antitubercular therapy. Silicosis features upper-lobe nodular fibrosis without organisms. The two coexist as silicotuberculosis in 3-30% of silicosis cases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lung Cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lung cancer presents as a discrete mass with possible cavitation, lymphadenopathy, and abnormal histology on biopsy. Silicosis produces multiple small nodules with characteristic upper-lobe distribution; lung cancer risk is doubled in silicosis patients, especially smokers, so biopsy of any new dominant mass is essential.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mesothelioma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mesothelioma is an asbestos-related pleural malignancy with pleural thickening and effusion. Silicosis affects the lung parenchyma with nodular fibrosis. Occupational history and imaging distinguish them; both involve dust-related occupational lung disease.

relatedConditions.name: Asbestosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another major pneumoconiosis caused by occupational inhalation of mineral fibres; both produce progressive fibrosis but with distinct imaging patterns.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Silicosis raises TB risk 3-30 fold; annual screening and treatment of latent TB are essential. Silicotuberculosis is a major cause of mortality in silicosis.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
relatedConditions.relevance: Silica exposure and concurrent smoking commonly produce overlap of silicosis and COPD; both contribute to lung function impairment.

relatedConditions.name: Lung Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Crystalline silica is an IARC Group 1 carcinogen; lung cancer risk is doubled in silicosis patients, synergistically amplified by smoking.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Silica exposure raises rheumatoid arthritis incidence; Caplan syndrome describes rheumatoid lung nodules in silica-exposed workers.

faqs.question: What is silicosis?
faqs.answer: Silicosis is an incurable lung disease caused by inhalation of respirable crystalline silica dust at work, leading to progressive nodular pulmonary fibrosis. It typically presents with breathlessness, dry cough, and fatigue years after exposure and substantially raises the risk of tuberculosis, lung cancer, and autoimmune disease.

faqs.question: What causes silicosis?
faqs.answer: Silicosis is caused by inhalation of fine crystalline silica particles (mostly α-quartz) in workplaces such as stone-cutting, quarrying, mining, sandblasting, foundry work, construction, ceramics, and especially engineered-stone fabrication. Particles smaller than 5 microns deposit in the alveoli and drive nodular fibrosis.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of silicosis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include progressive exertional breathlessness, persistent dry cough, fatigue, weight loss, chest pain, and reduced exercise tolerance. Many patients are asymptomatic in early disease and are detected on routine occupational health chest imaging.

faqs.question: How is silicosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a credible occupational silica exposure history, characteristic imaging changes (upper-lobe small rounded opacities on chest radiograph or HRCT), and exclusion of mimicking diseases. ILO classification of radiographs and high-resolution CT are central to assessment.

faqs.question: Can silicosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Silicosis cannot be cured; the pulmonary fibrosis is irreversible. Treatment focuses on stopping further silica exposure, managing complications such as tuberculosis and lung cancer, pulmonary rehabilitation, and supportive care. Lung transplantation is considered for end-stage disease.

faqs.question: What is engineered-stone silicosis?
faqs.answer: Engineered-stone silicosis is a rapidly accelerated form of silicosis caused by occupational exposure during fabrication of artificial quartz benchtops, which contain 70-95% crystalline silica. Cases have been reported in workers under 40 after only 3-10 years of exposure. Many jurisdictions are now banning dry processing of artificial stone.

faqs.question: How can silicosis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Prevention combines elimination or substitution of silica materials, engineering controls (water suppression, exhaust ventilation, enclosure), administrative controls (training, rotation, monitoring), and respiratory protection. Annual medical surveillance and tuberculosis screening detect early disease.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with silicosis?
faqs.answer: Life expectancy depends on disease form and complications. Chronic simple silicosis without progression may allow near-normal lifespan. Progressive massive fibrosis has 5-year survival of 50-70%. Accelerated and acute silicosis have markedly worse prognosis. Tuberculosis and lung cancer further reduce survival.

faqs.question: Does silicosis cause tuberculosis?
faqs.answer: Silicosis substantially raises tuberculosis risk by impairing alveolar macrophage function. Risk is 3-30 fold higher than the general population. Annual tuberculosis screening with interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) and treatment of latent TB are essential.

faqs.question: Is silicosis a form of cancer?
faqs.answer: Silicosis itself is not cancer, but crystalline silica is an IARC Group 1 carcinogen. Silicosis patients have approximately double the risk of lung cancer, synergistically increased by smoking. Low-dose CT screening is considered in eligible high-risk patients.

faqs.question: Can silicosis affect women?
faqs.answer: Yes, although men account for over 90% of recorded cases because of male-dominated workforces in mining, stone-cutting, and construction. Female workers in these industries are at the same risk per unit exposure and the increasing engineered-stone industry employs women in fabrication shops.

faqs.question: What is progressive massive fibrosis?
faqs.answer: Progressive massive fibrosis (PMF) describes coalescence of silicotic nodules into masses larger than 1 cm, typically in the upper lobes with surrounding emphysema. PMF causes severe restrictive lung disease, traction bronchiectasis, pulmonary hypertension, and respiratory failure.

faqs.question: Can I work after a diagnosis of silicosis?
faqs.answer: Permanent removal from silica-exposed work is essential. Alternative employment in dust-free settings is encouraged. Occupational medicine and social work teams support transition; many jurisdictions provide compensation through workers' compensation or social security schemes.

faqs.question: What is silicotuberculosis?
faqs.answer: Silicotuberculosis is active tuberculosis occurring on a background of silicosis. It can present atypically with rapidly progressive cavities, lobar collapse, or extensive disease. Treatment uses standard antitubercular regimens with close follow-up and consideration of extended duration.

faqs.question: Can silicosis cause autoimmune disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Silica exposure raises the risk of rheumatoid arthritis, scleroderma (systemic sclerosis), ANCA-associated vasculitis, and lupus. Caplan syndrome describes rheumatoid lung nodules in silica-exposed workers with rheumatoid arthritis. Periodic autoimmune screening is recommended.

faqs.question: Is lung transplantation an option in silicosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lung transplantation is considered for selected patients with end-stage silicosis and respiratory failure, no major comorbidity, and good functional reserve. Bilateral transplantation is preferred. Five-year survival is approximately 50-60% in published series.

faqs.question: What is the OSHA silica exposure limit?
faqs.answer: OSHA's permissible exposure limit (PEL) for respirable crystalline silica is 0.05 mg/m³ averaged over an 8-hour shift, with an action level of 0.025 mg/m³. NIOSH recommends an even lower limit of 0.025 mg/m³. Many countries adopt similar regulatory thresholds.

faqs.question: Can I claim compensation for silicosis?
faqs.answer: In most countries silicosis is compensable as an occupational disease through workers' compensation, social security, or veterans' affairs schemes. Documentation by an occupational medicine physician with imaging, lung function tests, and a detailed occupational history is essential.

faqs.question: How is silicosis monitored?
faqs.answer: Patients are followed every 6-12 months with chest imaging, spirometry with gas transfer (DLco), tuberculosis screening, autoimmune screening, and exercise testing. High-resolution CT every 2-5 years and selected lung cancer screening are considered. Specialist review continues lifelong.

faqs.question: Are there new treatments for silicosis?
faqs.answer: Antifibrotic drugs (pirfenidone and nintedanib) used in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis are being studied in progressive fibrosing silicosis, with limited evidence. Emerging trials of immunomodulators and macrophage-targeting therapy are ongoing. Lung transplantation remains the only definitive option for end-stage disease.
